meta.title: Nephrology Literature Update
meta.query: Clinical nephrology — RCTs, systematic reviews & meta-analyses (humans, English)
meta.source: Generated by Google NotebookLM, grounded in PubMed (NLM) abstracts. Each entry links to its PubMed record and DOI.
meta.note: Summaries and PICO are NotebookLM analyses of the source abstracts; criteria marked “not specified” were not stated.

articles.authors: Cianciolo G, Vetrano D, Tomassetti A, Tringali E, Iacoviello M et al.
articles.title: Efficacy of ARNIs on hard renal outcomes in heart failure with and without chronic kidney disease: when endpoint definition matters-an updated meta-analysis.
articles.summary: Sacubitril/valsartan was associated with a lower risk of sustained 50% eGFR decline (RR 0.68, 95% CI 0.57-0.82) and composite kidney outcomes (RR 0.70, 95% CI 0.58-0.84). It also significantly slowed annualized eGFR decline by a mean difference of 0.52 mL/min/1.73 m² per year (95% CI 0.35-0.69) .

pico.population: Adults with heart failure with or without chronic kidney disease
pico.comparison: Angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, angiotensin receptor blockers, or standard care
pico.outcome: Sustained ≥50% eGFR decline, end-stage kidney disease (ESKD), composite kidney outcomes, and annualized eGFR decline

appraisal.bottom_line: Sacubitril/valsartan reduces the risk of sustained 50% eGFR decline (RR 0.68) and composite renal events in patients with heart failure .
appraisal.relevance: Provides robust evidence for the nephroprotective effects of ARNIs in cardiorenal syndrome management .
appraisal.caveat: The association for isolated ESKD was directionally favorable but did not reach statistical significance (RR 0.80, 95% CI 0.64-1.00) .

articles.authors: Elganyny MKE, Elganyni AKE, Maskji HA, Ajaka N, Mishriki AES et al.
articles.title: Long-Term Safety and Renal Outcomes of Semaglutide in Non-Diabetic Obesity with Chronic Kidney Disease or Hypertension: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
articles.summary: Semaglutide treatment significantly reduced BMI (mean reduction ranging from 1.5% to 18.3%) and improved blood pressure control (SBP <130 mmHg). Renal function also improved, with significant increases in the proportion of patients achieving eGFR >30 mL/min/1.73m² (P < 0.001) .

pico.population: Non-diabetic patients with obesity and chronic kidney disease (CKD) or hypertension
pico.outcome: Body mass index (BMI), systolic blood pressure (SBP), eGFR, and albuminuria

inclusion: Studies on non-diabetic individuals with obesity and CKD or hypertension

appraisal.bottom_line: Semaglutide significantly reduces BMI and improves blood pressure and renal parameters (eGFR >30) in non-diabetic CKD populations .
appraisal.relevance: Supports the use of GLP-1 receptor agonists for renal protection and weight management in patients with CKD who do not have diabetes .
appraisal.caveat: The analysis was based on only three eligible studies with a relatively small total sample size (n=430) .

articles.authors: Kayler LK, Koizumi N, Nie J, Keller M, Cadzow RB et al.
articles.title: Randomized Controlled Trial of a Digital Intervention (KidneyTIME) to Promote Live Donor Kidney Transplantation.
articles.summary: In the intent-to-treat population, the KidneyTIME digital intervention did not significantly increase donor inquiries compared to active control. However, an as-treated analysis found that participants who viewed at least one video had a significantly higher cumulative donor inquiry probability (22.9% vs. 9.8%, p = 0.029) .

pico.population: Adult kidney transplant candidates in referral, evaluation, or listed phases
pico.intervention: KidneyTIME (animated video LDKT education and outreach enablement via video sharing)
pico.outcome: Primary: ≥1 live donor inquiry within 12 months; Secondary: LDKT knowledge, attitudes, and listing behaviors

appraisal.bottom_line: The KidneyTIME digital intervention did not increase donor inquiries in the primary ITT analysis but showed efficacy among those who actually engaged with the content .
appraisal.relevance: Digital educational tools can improve transplant knowledge and listing behaviors (IRR 1.62) but require better engagement strategies to translate into live donor inquiries .
appraisal.caveat: The primary outcome was only achieved in the planned as-treated analysis, indicating a strong dose-response effect but lack of overall intervention impact .

articles.authors: Uno C, Nishioka S, Tsuchida Y, Kabasawa H, Doi E et al.
articles.title: Combination effect of nutrition and exercise intervention in patients with maintenance dialysis: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.summary: Combined nutrition and exercise therapy significantly improved the 6-minute walk test distance (MD 45.17 m, 95% CI 12.60–77.75) and gait speed (MD 0.09 m/s, 95% CI 0.02 to 0.17) in hemodialysis patients. No significant differences were observed in BMI, lean body mass, or quality of life .

pico.population: Adults (≥18 years) receiving maintenance hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis
pico.intervention: Combined nutrition (mainly oral supplements) and exercise (resistance, aerobic, or neuromuscular electrical stimulation)
pico.comparison: Nutritional intervention alone, exercise alone, or no intervention
pico.outcome: Physical performance, skeletal muscle mass, BMI, dietary intake, quality of life (QOL), and mortality

inclusion: Studies comparing combined therapy to single or no intervention

appraisal.bottom_line: Combined nutrition and exercise therapy significantly improves physical performance metrics like walking distance and gait speed in hemodialysis patients .
appraisal.relevance: Supports a multidisciplinary approach to rehabilitation for dialysis patients to address functional decline .
appraisal.caveat: The certainty of evidence was rated as very low for all outcomes, and no clear benefit was seen for body composition or quality of life .

articles.authors: Wang Z, Pei J, Sun T, Ge S, Wu T et al.
articles.title: Cardiovascular-kidney-metabolic syndrome stage modifies the efficacy of intensive blood pressure control on cognitive outcomes: A post hoc analysis of SPRINT MIND.
articles.study_type: Post hoc analysis of a randomized controlled trial
articles.summary: Among hypertensive patients with non-advanced CKM (Stage 2), intensive BP control (<120 mmHg) significantly reduced the risk of probable dementia (HR 0.62, 95% CI 0.46–0.85). In contrast, no cognitive benefit was observed for those in advanced CKM stages 3–4 (HR 1.15), with a significant treatment-by-stage interaction (p = 0.009) .

pico.population: Participants from SPRINT MIND classified into Stage 2 (non-advanced) or Stages 3-4 (advanced) CKM syndrome
pico.intervention: Intensive blood pressure control (target systolic BP <120 mmHg)
pico.comparison: Standard blood pressure control (target systolic BP <140 mmHg)
pico.outcome: Primary: probable dementia; Secondary: mild cognitive impairment (MCI) or a composite

inclusion: Participants of the Systolic Blood Pressure Intervention Trial - Memory and Cognition in Decreased Hypertension (SPRINT MIND)

appraisal.bottom_line: Intensive blood pressure control (<120 mmHg) reduces dementia risk by 38% in early-stage CKM but provides no cognitive benefit in patients with advanced disease .
appraisal.relevance: Underscores the need for early aggressive blood pressure management to preserve cognitive function before patients reach advanced renal or metabolic stages .
appraisal.caveat: This was a post hoc analysis, which is inherently hypothesis-generating rather than definitive .

articles.authors: Bui HT, Apriliyasari RW, Bui QHQ, Tsai PS et al.
articles.title: Association Between Frailty and Cognitive Impairment in Chronic Kidney Disease: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
articles.summary: Meta-analysis of 17 studies involving 129,868 patients found that frail CKD patients have significantly higher odds of cognitive impairment than robust CKD patients (OR 3.13, 95% CI 1.92–5.12). Heterogeneity was driven by the region of the study and frailty measurement methods .
inclusion: Cross-sectional, cohort, case-control, randomized controlled trials, and quasi-experimental studies
Reported association between frailty and cognitive impairment in CKD

appraisal.bottom_line: Frail patients with CKD are over three times more likely to have cognitive impairment than their non-frail counterparts .
appraisal.relevance: Practitioners should implement frailty screening to identify CKD patients at high risk for cognitive decline for targeted interventions .
appraisal.caveat: The included studies were mostly observational (cross-sectional and cohort), so a causal relationship cannot be definitively established .

articles.authors: Kueh MTW, Yeo JKW, Chen Y, Chua NYZ, Koh HL et al.
articles.title: Global Prevalence, Incidence, and Outcomes of Coexisting MASLD and Chronic Kidney Disease: A Meta-Analysis.
articles.summary: The global prevalence of CKD in patients with MASLD is 15.22% (95% CI 9.69–23.12). Coexisting CKD in MASLD patients doubles the all-cause mortality rate compared to MASLD alone (18.28 vs. 7.26 per 1000 person-years) .

pico.population: Adults (≥18 years) with Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD)
pico.outcome: CKD prevalence and incidence, all-cause mortality, and cardiovascular outcomes

inclusion: Observational studies reporting CKD prevalence and/or incidence in MASLD adults

appraisal.bottom_line: CKD affects one in seven individuals with MASLD and is associated with a two-fold increase in all-cause mortality .
appraisal.relevance: Nephrologists and hepatologists should coordinate for earlier kidney assessment and integrated care in this high-risk metabolic population .
appraisal.caveat: The meta-analysis relied on observational studies, and there was significant variability in prevalence estimates .

articles.authors: Schildt A, Rautalin I, Vasankari V, Korja M, Huhtakangas J.
articles.title: Cost-Effectiveness of Intracranial Aneurysm Screening: A Systematic Review.
articles.summary: A review of 15 modelling studies showed that screening for unruptured intracranial aneurysms (UIAs) was consistently cost-effective for high-risk populations, including those with autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD). All 10 studies evaluating high-risk groups reported screening as either cost-effective or cost-saving .

pico.population: High-risk populations (ADPKD, bicuspid aortic valve, family history, etc.) or the general population
pico.outcome: Economic outcomes including cost-effectiveness (ICERs) or cost-savings

inclusion: Studies reporting economic analyses of UIA screening
Models assessing general or increased-risk populations

appraisal.bottom_line: Screening for intracranial aneurysms is cost-effective in high-risk patients, including those with ADPKD .
appraisal.relevance: Provides economic justification for specialized screening protocols in ADPKD patients to prevent subarachnoid hemorrhage .
appraisal.caveat: The quality of all included studies was rated as low, and results depend heavily on model input parameters like assumed annual rupture rates .
